front: What is the primary purpose of the Data Link Layer in the OSI model?
back: The Data Link Layer is responsible for the node-to-node delivery of data frames across a shared physical medium. Its primary purposes include framing, physical addressing (MAC addresses), error control (detecting/correcting bit errors), flow control, and access control to the medium.

front: What is the difference between Stop-and-Wait ARQ and Go-Back-N ARQ protocols?
back: Stop-and-Wait ARQ is a simple protocol where the sender transmits one frame and waits for an acknowledgment (ACK) before sending the next. Go-Back-N ARQ allows the sender to have multiple frames (up to a window size, N) outstanding. If a frame is lost, the sender goes back and retransmits that frame and all subsequent frames.

front: What is the function of the Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) in the Data Link Layer?
back: CRC is an error-detection code used to detect accidental changes to raw data. The sender performs a calculation on the data bits to generate a CRC code, which is appended to the frame. The receiver performs the same calculation; if the result doesn't match the received CRC code, an error is detected.

front: What is the main purpose of the Media Access Control (MAC) sublayer?
back: The MAC sublayer is responsible for controlling how devices in a network gain access to the transmission medium (channel access). It defines procedures to coordinate transmission and avoid collisions, using protocols like CSMA/CD (for Ethernet) or CSMA/CA (for WiFi).

front: What is the key difference between a hub and a switch at the Data Link Layer?
back: A hub is a physical layer device that simply repeats incoming signals to all other ports, operating in a single collision domain. A switch is an intelligent Data Link Layer (Layer 2) device that uses MAC addresses to forward frames only to the specific port where the destination device is located, creating separate collision domains for each port.
